note:
周二，苹果发布了一系列新产品，包括两款新的苹果手表，两款新的iPad，一项新的健身订阅服务，以及它的一系列服务。
几乎可以肯定的是，这不是该公司计划的首次亮相。苹果喜欢在今年最大的活动-新款iPhone的发布会上宣扬过去一年在芯片设计方面的创新。九年前，A系列芯片在iPhone1之外首次亮相。
但事实证明，2020年非常非常擅长扰乱计划。虽然iPad Air已经准备好发布，但iPhone 12却没有发布。因此，苹果发布了A14处理器，并吹嘘自己是如何让新的iPad Air比之前的机型快得多的。它的速度提高了40%，图形性能提高了30%，机器学习性能提高了10倍。
但所有这些数据都将iPad Air中的A14与之前型号的A12处理器进行了比较，而不是iPhone 11系列中的A13处理器。现在，苹果可能会声称，对屏幕尺寸和散热特性相似的设备进行比较才是公平的。但是，在一丝不苟地坚持与A12进行比较的同时，苹果并没有告诉我们，基础A14处理器-可能是下一代iPad Pro机型的基础，甚至可能是第一轮运行苹果硅(Apple Silicon)的Mac-与其上一代处理器相比快了多少。
我不认为苹果这样做是因为它不以A14为荣。(相反，苹果似乎非常清楚这款芯片的重要性，包括它是苹果第一款采用台积电5纳米新工艺制造的芯片。)。不，这是为了给苹果即将发布的iPhone发布会留出一些空间，让他们更多地吹嘘A14。这就说得通了。IPhone是苹果最重要的产品。它值得吹嘘一下。
与此同时，我们只剩下对A14与A13相比的纯粹猜测。去年我将iPhone 11与之前的机型进行了对比测试，结果显示单核CPU性能提升了29%，多核CPU性能提升了55%。如果A14中的单个高性能内核比A12快40%，这意味着它在单核操作中可能会快10%？想必多核性能会更好，但我们就是不知道。
这些天来，性能的最大飞跃似乎发生在机器学习任务中，苹果将神经引擎的核心数量翻了一番，从8个增加到16个，CPU本身的机器学习加速器数量也增加了一倍(它允许在ML任务中使用优化的矩阵乘法)。
A14还有没有其他我们不知道的优化？哦，可能吧。考虑到新款iPhone可能会配备升级的摄像头，其中一些会包括激光雷达传感器(在2020年的iPad Pro上推出-iPad今年真的抢了iPhone的风头)。这些摄像头和传感器可能会利用苹果公司在A14上加入的一些非常具体的秘密酱油，在iPhone出现在阳光下之前，苹果公司不会透露任何关于这一点的信息。
所以，就目前而言，我们知道的是有一款新的iPad Air，它配备了A14处理器，A14处理器在很多方面比A12更好，在我们还不太清楚的方面比A13更好。当新的iPhone发布时，我们会了解更多，当新的iPad Pro型号与放大版的A14一起出现时，我们可能会了解更多。
至于配备苹果硅片的Mac电脑呢？谁知道呢。第一款苹果硅Mac可能会搭载与iPad Pro和苹果硅开发者过渡套件(Apple Silicon Developer Transition Kit)中相同的A12Z一样适中的东西。它可以配备专为Mac笔记本电脑和iPad Pro设计的A14X处理器。或者它可以附带完全不同的东西-尽管即使那样，我打赌它将是一个基于A14的芯片，即使它有一个完全不同的名字。
IPad2在A5处理器出现在iPhone4S之前几个月就推出了A5处理器。--↩。

note_en:
On Tuesday Apple unveiled a bunch of new stuff, including two new Apple Watches, two new iPads, a new fitness subscription service, and a bundle of its many services
  It was almost certainly not the debut that the company had planned. Apple likes to trumpet the innovations in chip design it has made over the past year at its biggest event of the year, the launch of the new iPhones. It’s been nine years since an A-series chip has debuted outside of the iPhone  1.
 But 2020 has proven to be really,  really good at disrupting plans. And while the iPad Air was ready to be announced, the iPhone 12 wasn’t. So Apple unveiled the A14 processor and boasted about how it made the new iPad Air so much faster than the previous model. It’s up to 40% faster, with 30% faster graphics performance, and machine-learning performance is up to 10 times faster.
 But all of those figures compare the A14 in the iPad Air to the A12 processor in the previous model, not the A13 processor found in the iPhone 11 series. Now, Apple would likely claim that it’s only fair to make a comparison across devices with similar screen sizes and thermal characteristics. But in scrupulously adhering to the comparisons to the A12, Apple is not telling us how much faster the base A14 processor—likely the foundation of the next generation of iPad Pro models and possibly even the first round of Macs running Apple Silicon—is compared to its immediate predecessor.
 I don’t think Apple’s doing this because it’s not proud of the A14. (On the contrary, Apple seems very aware of how important this chip is, including the fact that it’s Apple’s first to be manufactured using Taiwan Semiconductor’s new 5-nanometer process.) No, this is about leaving some space for Apple’s forthcoming iPhone launch event to boast a bit more about the A14. Which makes sense. The iPhone  is Apple’s most important product. It deserves to be boasted about a bit.
 In the meantime, we’re left with pure speculation about how the A14 compares to the A13. Last year  I tested the iPhone 11 against previous models, and the results showed a 29% improvement in single-core CPU performance and a 55% improvement in multi-core CPU performance. If a single high-performance core in the A14 is 40% faster than that of the A12, that would suggest that it would be maybe ten percent faster at single core operations? Presumably multi-core performance would be greater, but we just don’t know.
  The biggest jumps in performance these days seem to be happening in machine-learning tasks, where Apple has doubled the Neural Engine’s core count from eight to sixteen, as well as double the number of machine-learning accelerators (which allow optimized matrix multiplication used in ML tasks) in the CPU itself.
 Are there other optimizations in the A14 that we don’t know about yet? Oh, probably. Consider that the new iPhones will likely feature upgraded cameras and some of them will include LIDAR sensors (introduced on the 2020 iPad Pro—the iPad’s really stealing the iPhone’s thunder this year). Those cameras and sensors will probably be taking advantage of some very specific secret sauce Apple has ladled into the A14, and Apple isn’t going to say a peep about any of that until the iPhone gets its moment in the sun.
 So, for now, what we know is that there’s a new iPad Air, it’s got an A14 processor, that A14 processor is better than the A12 in a bunch of ways and better than the A13 in ways we don’t really know yet. We’ll learn more when the new iPhones are announced, and we’ll presumably learn even more when new iPad Pro models appear with a scaled-up version of the A14.
 And as for Macs with Apple silicon? Who knows. The first Apple silicon Mac could ship with something as modest as the same A12Z that’s in the iPad Pro and the Apple silicon Developer Transition Kit. It could ship with an A14X processor designed for Mac laptops and iPad Pros alike. Or it could ship with something completely different—though even then, I’d lay odds that it will be a chip based on the A14, even if it’s got an entirely different name.
 The iPad 2 introduced the A5 processor months before it appeared in the iPhone 4S.  ↩
  If you appreciate articles like this one, support us by  becoming a Six Colors subscriber. Subscribers get access to an exclusive podcast, members-only stories, and a special community.
title: A14处理器不情愿的首次亮相
title_en: The reluctant debut of the A14 processor

我决定试着做一些骷髅叶。所以我做了一些谷歌搜索，并决定尝试这种方法。
我们晚上出去，从当地的灌木丛里摘了一些树叶。完全不可疑的🙂。
我在易趣上买了一些氢氧化钠和一个便宜的钢锅。注：它不能是铝，因为氢氧化钠会与铝反应！
虽然氢氧化钠不是致命的毒药，但你真的不想让它沾到你的皮肤或眼睛里，所以手套/护目镜是安全的必需品。嗯，我真的应该找一件便宜的实验室大衣来保护我的衣服，以防这类东西：
我把比例放大到1升(爱丁堡)自来水和30克氢氧化钠粉末。我把它们放进锅里，煮到沸腾，然后加入叶子。
为了好玩，我还用我的新pH纸(也是从eBay/China买的)测试了溶液的pH值。大概是14度，太碱性了！
说明书建议煮大约两个小时，但这似乎取决于你选择的叶子。我大约每隔20分钟检查一次，等叶子准备好了就把它们拔出来。
托盘2：用一些“普通家用漂白剂”(也就是次氯酸钠)将残留的颜色漂白掉。
第二天早上，我能够剥开更结实的叶子，给我带来了这些：
你需要使用坚固的树叶。我试了试一些荨麻叶，但它们很快就变成了糊状。一些树叶看起来加工得很好，但结果发现太脆弱了，干燥后无法从箔片上取下：这当然取决于物种。也许会有更好的方法来擦干它们，我会考虑的。
一次只能处理一种叶子，否则你必须不断地检查花盆里的每一种叶子，这意味着你要更多地干扰它们进行检查。
它可真是笨手笨脚的！在加工过程中，你必须戴着手套小心地用手展开树叶，使它们在晾干之前变平。
我试着处理了一片干燥的橡树叶，因为理论上它应该更接近于骨架：它似乎效果不是很好(你可以在铝箔照片上看到不成功的结果)。
它们肯定比我预期的更结实，但它们仍然相当精致。我想试着把它们染色，然后把它们嵌入一些透明的树脂中。
note_en:
I decided I wanted to try making some skeletonised leaves. So I did some Googling and decided to try  this approach.
 We went out in the evening and gathered some leaves from the local Shrubbery. Totally not suspicious 🙂
  I bought some Sodium Hydroxide and a cheap steel pot from ebay. Note: it must  not be Aluminium as the Sodium Hydroxide will react with Aluminium!
  Although Sodium Hydroxide isn’t a deadly poison, you really don’t want it on your skin or in your eyes, so gloves/goggles are a necessity for safety. Hmm, I should really look into some sort of cheap lab coat as well to protect my clothes for this sorta stuff:
  I scaled up the proportions to 1L of (Edinburgh) tap water and 30G of Sodium Hydroxide powder. I put them in the pot, brought it to the boil and added the leaves.
 For fun I also tested the pH of the solution with my new pH paper (also from Ebay/China). Its about a 14, so pretty alkaline!
  The instructions suggested boiling for about two hours, but it appears to depend on the leaves you choose. I checked on it every 20 minutes or so, and pulled leaves out as they became ready.
  Tray 2: Some “Ordinary Household Bleach” (aka Sodium Hypochlorite) to bleach any remaining colour out.
   The next morning I was able to unpeel the more robust leaves, yielding me these:
   You need to use  robust leaves from trees. I tried some nettle leaves, but they quickly turned to mush. Some of the tree leaves appeared to process fine, but turned out to be way too delicate to remove from the foil after drying: definitely depends on the species. There may be a better way to dry them, will think on it.
 Only process one species of leaf at a time, otherwise you constantly have to check each one in the pot, which means you’re disturbing them more to check.
  Its fiddly! During processing, you have to  carefully unroll the leaves by hand while wearing gloves to get them flat prior to drying.
 I tried processing a dried up Oak leaf since theoretically it should be closer to being skeletonised: it didn’t seem to work very well (you can see the unsuccessful result on the aluminium foil photo).
  They’re definitely more robust than I expected, but they’re still quite delicate. I fancy trying dying them and embedding them into some transparent resin next.
title: 制作骷髅叶

note:
不久前，我发表了一系列文章，探讨了SCons的可伸缩性，这是一种流行的基于Python的构建工具。简而言之，我的实验表明，随着目标数量的增加，SCons在构建运行时表现出大致的二次增长：
最近，Dirk Baechle试图反驳我在SCons维基上的一个条目中的发现：为什么SCons不慢。我认为德克提出了一些可信的建议，可以解释我的结果，他做了一些聪明的事情，努力让我的结果无效。不幸的是，他的方法有缺陷，他的结论是无效的。我最初的结果仍然有效：SCons真的很慢。在接下来的部分中，我将分享我自己更新的基准，并展示Dirk的分析哪里出错。
和前面一样，我使用genscons.pl生成了从2,000到50,000个目标的示例构建。然而，这一次我的测试系统要强大得多：
在运行测试之前，我重新启动了系统，以确保没有消耗内存或CPU的流氓进程。我还强制CPU内核进入“性能”模式，以确保它们以1.7 GHz的全速运行，而不是以空闲时切换到的较低933 MHz运行。
我认为德克有两个可信的理论来解释我在最初测试中获得的结果。首先，Dirk想知道这些结果是否可能是虚拟内存交换的结果-我最初的测试系统的RAM相对较少，而SCons本身使用了大量内存。物理内存耗尽，迫使操作系统将内存交换到磁盘，这似乎是有道理的。正如Dirk所说，“这将解释构建时间的增加”--当然会！当我最初运行这些测试时，我不记得看到任何内存交换的迹象，但老实说，那是近4年前的事了，也许我的记忆不可靠。为了消除这种可能性，这次我在具有16 GB RAM的系统上运行了测试。在测试期间，我运行了vmstat5，它每隔5秒收集内存和交换使用信息，并在日志中捕获结果。
接下来，他建议我通过指示SCons继承环境环境来歪曲结果，而不是使用SCons默认的“净化”环境。也就是说，他认为我应该使用env=Environment()而不是env=Environment(env=os.environ)。为了确保这不是一个因素，我修改了测试，使它们不会继承环境。同时，为了加快测试速度，我用echo代替了编译器和其他命令。此外，我对编译器的基准测试不感兴趣--只对SCons感兴趣！下面是我的环境声明现在的样子：
有了这些更改，我重新运行了我的基准。不出所料，结果没有变化。毫无疑问：SCons不是线性扩展的。相反，增长是多项式的，遵循n1.85曲线。多亏了vmstat的输出，我们可以确定绝对没有影响基准的交换。下面是结果的图表，其中包括一条n1.85曲线以供比较-请注意，您几乎看不到该曲线，因为它与观察到的数据非常匹配！
为了进行比较，我使用SCons构建日志创建了一个shell脚本，该脚本执行相同系列的echo命令。对于50,000个目标，shell脚本运行时间为1.097秒。你没看错：1.097。诚然，shell脚本不会执行最新检查等工作，但在平均3759个SCons运行时中，3758个(99.97%)仍然是SCons开销。
我还创建了一个非递归Makefile，它使用相同的ECHO命令“构建”相同的目标。这是与SCon更现实的比较-毕竟，没有人会梦想用直线shell脚本实际控制构建，但很多人会使用GNU make来做这件事。在50,000个目标的情况下，GNU Make的运行速度为82.469秒-比SCons快45倍以上。
如果性能问题如此明显，为什么德克没有看到呢？这是根据他的测试结果绘制的图表：
德克说，这证明了“SCons的线性缩放”。我觉得这一说法令人费解，因为他的数据清楚地表明SCons不是线性扩展的。这真的很简单：线性缩放只是意味着您添加的每个新目标的构建时间都会增加相同的量，而不管您已经有多少个目标。换句话说，这意味着1,000个目标与2,000个目标之间的构建时间差异与10,000个目标与11,000个目标之间或30,000个目标与31,000个目标之间的构建时间差异完全相同。或者，换一种说法，这意味着当您绘制构建时间与目标数量的关系图时，您应该得到一条直线，任何点的斜率都没有变化。现在你告诉我：这描述了德克的图表吗？
下面是该图的另一个版本，这一次增加了几条附加线，显示了如果SCons真的线性缩放，该图会是什么样子。第一个预测基于从2,500个目标到4,500个目标的原始图形-也就是说，如果我们假设SCons呈线性扩展，并且2,500个和4,500个目标之间构建时间的增加代表了增加2,000个目标的成本，那么这条线向我们显示了我们应该如何预期构建时间会增加。类似地，第二个投影基于4,500到8,500个目标之间的原始图形。您可以很容易地看到，实际数据与这两个投影中的任何一个都不匹配。此外，您可以看到这些投影的斜率正在增加：
这表明了当您试图根据经验数据表征系统的可伸缩性时，大规模测试的重要性。在低尺度下很难区分多项式与对数或线性，特别是在加入常量因子的情况下-对于小输入，多项式算法有时甚至可以比线性算法提供更好的绝对性能！直到你用足够大的值绘制足够多的数据点，就像我已经做过的那样，才能很容易地看到和识别这条曲线。
接下来，Dirk在分析器下重新运行他的一些测试，假设如果发现性能问题，它将在分析数据中表现出来-当然，至少有一个函数会在运行时表现出比预期更大的增长。Dirk只在8,500和16,500个目标上共享了两个运行的概要数据，这两个运行都是增量构建。这是不幸的，有几个原因。首先，增量构建上的性能问题不如完整构建上的明显。其次，由于只有两个数据点，从字面上不可能确定增长是线性的还是多项式的。德克的分析结果是否定的：他在任何功能上都没有发现“显著的变化或增加”。
幸运的是，我自己做这个实验很容易。Dirk使用了内置到Python中的cProfile。要分析Python脚本，可以从命令行注入cProfile，如下所示：python-m cProfile scons。就在Python退出之前，cProfile转储运行期间调用的每个函数的计时数据。我在启用分析器的情况下运行了几个完整的构建，从2,000个目标到20,000个目标。然后，我按函数内部时间(独占地花费在函数中的时间，而不是在其子代中花费的时间)对性能分析数据进行排序。在每次运行中，相同的两个函数出现在列表的顶部：posx.waitpid和posx.fork。老实说，这让我大吃一惊-以前我认为问题出在SCons的任务管理器实现上。但我真的不能反驳这些数据。SCons会将大部分时间花在运行和等待子进程执行上，甚至花在这些函数上的时间会随子进程数量的增加而增加，这是有道理的。但是看看这两个函数的运行时增长情况：
与总体构建时间一样，这些曲线显然是非线性的。有了这些知识，我又回到了德克的侧写数据中。令我惊讶的是，在Dirk的数据中甚至没有Pox.waitpid和posx.fork。仔细观察，他的数据似乎只包括所有函数的一个子集-大约600个函数，而我的分析数据包含1500多个函数。我无法解释这一点-也许Dirk过滤了结果以排除Python库中的函数，假设问题一定出在SCons自己的代码中，而不是在构建它的库中。
这演示了性能分析的第二个基本原则：确保考虑所有数据。程序员对性能问题的直觉是出了名的差--甚至是我的！--这就是为什么在行动之前进行测量很重要。但是，如果您遗漏了关键数据，或者如果您在进行任何分析之前丢弃了部分数据，那么测量就不会有任何帮助。
从表面上看，性能分析似乎应该很简单：启动计时器、运行一些代码、停止计时器。如果操作正确，性能分析可以照亮应用程序性能的阴暗角。做得不正确--有很多方法做得不对--可能会使您徒劳无功，并导致您浪费资源来修复错误的问题。
德克·贝克尔(Dirk Baechle)开始分析SCons的表现是出于好意，但他在分析过程中犯了一些错误，导致他得出了错误的结论。首先，他没有运行足够的大规模测试来真正看到性能问题。其次，他过滤实验数据的方式掩盖了问题的存在。但或许他最大的错误是从一个结论开始--即不存在性能问题--然后寻找数据来支持它，而不是从数据开始，让数据公正地引导他得出基于证据的结论。
对我来说，证据似乎是毋庸置疑的：随着构建目标数量的增加，SCons在运行时表现出大致的二次增长，使得其无法用于大规模软件开发(数万个构建输出)。没有证据表明这是虚拟内存交换的结果。分析表明，位置为.waitpid和位置为.fork的可能是一对罪魁祸首。我让Dirk和SCons团队进一步调查；同时，您可以在我的GitHub库中找到我的测试工具和测试结果。如果你能看到我的方法中的缺陷，请在评论中发声！
note_en:
A while back I posted a series of articles exploring the scalability of SCons, a popular Python-based build tool. In a nutshell, my experiments showed that  SCons exhibits roughly quadratic growth in build runtimes as the number of targets increases:
  Recently Dirk Baechle attempted to rebut my findings in an entry on the SCons wiki:  Why SCons is not slow. I thought Dirk made some credible suggestions that could explain my results, and he did some smart things in his effort to invalidate my results. Unfortunately, his methods were flawed and his conclusions are invalid. My original results still stand:  SCons really is slow. In the sections that follow I’ll share my own updated benchmarks and show where Dirk’s analysis went wrong.
 As before, I used  genscons.pl to generate sample builds ranging from 2,000 to 50,000 targets. However, my test system was much beefier this time:
  Before running the tests, I rebooted the system to ensure there were no rogue processes consuming memory or CPU. I also forced the CPU cores into “performance” mode to ensure that they ran at their full 1.7GHz speed, rather than at the lower 933MHz they switch to when idle.
 I think Dirk had two credible theories to explain the results I obtained in my original tests. First, Dirk wondered if those results may have been the result of  virtual memory swapping — my original test system had relatively little RAM, and SCons itself uses a lot of memory. It’s plausible that physical memory was exhausted, forcing the OS to swap memory to disk. As Dirk said, “this would explain the increase of build times” — you bet it would! I don’t remember seeing any indication of memory swapping when I ran these tests originally, but to be honest it was nearly 4 years ago and perhaps my memory is not reliable. To eliminate this possibility, I ran the tests on a system with 16 GB RAM this time. During the tests I ran   vmstat 5, which collects memory and swap usage information at five second intervals, and captured the result in a log.
 Next, he suggested that I skewed the results by directing SCons to inherit the ambient environment, rather than using SCons’ default “sanitized” environment. That is, he felt I should have used   env = Environment() rather than   env = Environment(ENV = os.environ). To ensure that this was not a factor, I modified the tests so that they did not inherit the environment. At the same time, I substituted   echo for the compiler and other commands, in order to make the tests faster. Besides, I’m not interested in benchmarking the compiler — just SCons! Here’s what my   Environment declaration looks like now:
  With these changes in place I reran my benchmarks. As expected, there was no change in the outcome. There is no doubt:  SCons does  not scale linearly. Instead the growth is  polynomial, following an n 1.85 curve. And thanks to the the  vmstat output we can be certain that there was absolutely no swapping affecting the benchmarks. Here’s a graph of the results, including an n 1.85 curve for comparison — notice that you can barely see that curve because it matches the observed data so well!
  For comparison, I used the SCons build log to make a shell script that executes the same series of   echo commands. At 50,000 targets, the shell script ran in 1.097s. You read that right:  1.097s. Granted, the shell script doesn’t do stuff like up-to-date checks, etc., but still — of the 3,759s average SCons runtime, 3,758s — 99.97% — is SCons overhead.
 I also created a non-recursive Makefile that “builds” the same targets with the same   echo commands. This is a more realistic comparison to SCons — after all, nobody would dream of actually controlling a build with a straight-line shell script, but lots of people would use GNU make to do it. With 50,000 targets, GNU make ran for  82.469s — more than 45 times faster than SCons.
  If the performance problems are so obvious, why did Dirk fail to see them? Here’s a graph made from his test results:
  Dirk says that this  demonstrates “SCons’ linear scaling”. I find this statement baffling, because his data clearly shows that  SCons does not scale linearly. It’s simple, really:  linear scaling just means that the build time increases by the same amount for each new target you add, regardless of how many targets you already have. Put another way, it means that the difference in build time between 1,000 targets and 2,000 targets is  exactly the same as the difference between 10,000 and 11,000 targets, or between 30,000 and 31,000 targets. Or, put yet another way, it means that when you plot the build time versus the number of targets, you should get a straight line with  no change in slope at any point. Now you tell me: does that describe Dirk’s graph?
 Here’s another version of that graph, this time augmented with a couple additional lines that show what the plot would look like if SCons were truly scaling linearly. The first projection is based on the original graph from 2,500 to 4,500 targets — that is, if we assume that SCons scales linearly and that the increase in build time between 2,500 and 4,500 targets is representative of the cost to add 2,000 more targets, then this line shows us how we should expect the build time to increase. Similarly, the second projection is based on the original graph between 4,500 and 8,500 targets. You can easily see that the actual data does not match either projection. Furthermore you can see that the slope of these projections is  increasing:
  This shows the importance of testing at large scale when you’re trying to characterize the scalability of a system from empirical data. It can be difficult to differentiate polynomial from logarithmic or linear at low scales, especially once you incorporate the constant factors — polynomial algorithms can sometimes even give  better absolute performance for small inputs than linear algorithms! It’s not until you plot enough data points at large enough values, as I’ve done, that it becomes easy to see and identify the curve.
 Next, Dirk reran some of his tests under a profiler, on the very reasonable assumption that if there was a performance problem to be found, it would manifest in the profiling data — surely at least one function would demonstrate a larger-than-expected growth in runtime. Dirk only shared profiling data for two runs, both incremental builds, at 8,500 and 16,500 targets. That’s unfortunate for a couple reasons. First, the performance problem is less apparent on incremental builds than on full builds. Second, with only two datapoints it is literally not possible to determine whether growth is linear or polynomial. The results of Dirk’s profiling was negative: he found no “significant difference or increase” in any function.
 Fortunately it’s easy to run this experiment myself. Dirk used  cProfile, which is built-in to Python. To profile a Python script you can inject cProfile from the command-line, like this:   python -m cProfile scons. Just before Python exits, cProfile dumps timing data for every function invoked during the run. I ran several full builds with the profiler enabled, from 2,000 to 20,000 targets. Then I sorted the profiling data by function internal time (time spent in the function exclusively, not in its descendents).  In every run, the same two functions appeared at the top of the list:   posix.waitpid and   posix.fork. To be honest this was a surprise to me — previously I believed the problem was in SCons’ Taskmaster implementation. But I can’t really argue with the data. It makes sense that SCons would spend most of its time running and waiting for child processes to execute, and even that the amount of time spent in these functions would increase as the number of child processes increases. But look at the growth in runtimes in these two functions:
  Like the overall build time, these curves are obviously non-linear. Armed with this knowledge, I went back to Dirk’s profiling data. To my surprise,  posix.waitpid and posix.fork don’t even appear in Dirk’s data. On closer inspection, his data seems to include only a subset of all functions — about 600 functions, whereas  my profiling data contains more than 1,500. I cannot explain this — perhaps Dirk filtered the results to exclude functions that are part of the Python library, assuming that the problem must be in SCons’ own code rather than in the library on which it is built.
 This demonstrates a second fundamental principle of performance analysis: make sure that you consider  all the data. Programmers’ intuition about performance problems is notoriously bad — even mine! — which is why it’s important to measure before acting. But measuring won’t help if you’re missing critical data or if you discard part of the data before doing any analysis.
 On the surface, performance analysis seems like it should be simple: start a timer, run some code, stop the timer. Done correctly, performance analysis can illuminate the dark corners of your application’s performance. Done incorrectly — and there are  many ways to do it incorrectly — it can lead you on a wild goose chase and cause you to squander resources fixing the wrong problems.
 Dirk Baechle had good intentions when he set out to analyze SCons performance, but he made some mistakes in his process that led him to an erroneous conclusion. First, he didn’t run enough large-scale tests to really see the performance problem. Second, he filtered his experimental data in a way that obscured the existence of the problem. But perhaps his worst mistake was to start with a conclusion — that there is no performance problem — and then look for data to support it, rather than starting with the data and letting it impartially guide him to an evidence-based conclusion.
 To me the evidence seems indisputable:  SCons exhibits roughly quadratic growth in runtimes as the number of build targets increases, rendering it unusable for large-scale software development (tens of thousands of build outputs). There is no evidence that this is a result of virtual memory swapping. Profiling suggests a possible pair of culprits in   posix.waitpid and   posix.fork. I leave it to Dirk and the SCons team to investigate further; in the meantime, you can find  my test harness and test results in my GitHub repo. If you can see a flaw in my methodology, sound off in the comments!
title: SCONS仍然很慢

note:
今年4月，在该州最大的购物中心之一的街道对面的新泽西州一个工薪阶层小镇上的犹太人公墓贝斯·伊斯雷尔(Beth以色列)，13名挖墓工埋葬了大约300具身体-其中许多人被工人推定为新冠肺炎的受害者。
在典型的一个月里，贝丝以色列公墓的工作人员会挖掘50到100个坟墓。在新泽西州和邻近的纽约市爆发冠状病毒的高峰期，病毒席卷了许多与贝丝以色列有联系的哈西德派犹太人社区，挖墓者在黎明时分前往田野，浇筑地基，铺设木材，挖掘战壕，举办葬礼，搬运棺材，并用挖掘机填满坟墓。据挖墓人工会卡车司机当地469说，掘墓人说他们经常轮班工作10个半小时，没有危险津贴，只有零星的洗手间和午餐休息时间。
“我这辈子从未见过这样的事情。六个星期以来，我们每天埋葬10到14次。“一位在贝丝以色列公司工作的掘墓人告诉”母板“。例如，Motherboard获得的一份埋葬时间表显示，4月份的一天，在四个半小时的时间里安排了10次埋葬和5次陵墓埋葬。工作人员说，有时在分配的时间内完成所有的埋葬是不可能的。
“我们的工作量如此之大，工人却如此之少，以至于我们不得不违背犹太传统，让人们的亲人在坟墓里敞开四七个小时，甚至是通宵，”他继续说。“我不认为家人知道这件事后会很感激。”
母板允许五名现任贝丝以色列挖墓人匿名，允许他们坦率地谈论自己的工作条件。掘墓人担心他们的雇主，一家名为StoneMor Corporation的公司，因为向媒体发表讲话而遭到报复。StoneMor是美国第二大公墓和殡仪馆公司，在27个州和波多黎各监管着321个公墓和90家殡仪馆。
贝斯以色列的挖墓工与卡车司机Local 469成立了工会，但尚未签署集体谈判合同，近几个月来，他们向国家劳动关系委员会(NLRB)提出了两项不公平劳动行为指控，并向职业安全与健康管理局(OSHA)提交了一份针对StoneMor的56页起诉书。根据主板审查的NLRB投诉，工人们将他们的一些工作转包给收入更低的工人，他们说这是非法的。
根据卡车司机当地的469，贝丝以色列的最低收入的掘墓人，其中许多是拉丁裔，赚取不到15美元每小时。据工会官员称，沿着这条路不到一英里，在30多年前工人成立工会的黎巴嫩山公墓，掘墓工每小时至少赚24.62美元。贝丝以色列公司的掘墓人很清楚工资差距。
“我周一到周五，每隔一个星期天都要工作，副业也做手工，生活还是不够的。我有四个年幼的孩子，“一位掘墓人告诉”母板“。“我是因为工会才来做这份工作的。我们本以为可以大幅加薪，但我们仍在观望事态发展。“
尽管与附近的墓地相比，工资差距很大，但在最好的情况下，贝丝以色列公司的工人仍然必须处理这项严峻的工作。主板公司获得的更多演示幻灯片和信息单解释说，工人们可能会接触到可能死于新冠肺炎的人的体液或组织，并解释说，坟墓可能会坍塌，工人死亡的最常见原因是挖掘。文件还解释说，贝斯以色列公司有特殊的地质条件，使得埋葬工作具有挑战性，比如地下水位高，坟墓会出现一种叫沸腾的现象，水向上渗入坟墓中。这份文件还解释说，贝斯以色列公司有一些特殊的地质条件，使得埋葬工作具有挑战性，比如地下水位很高，坟墓容易出现一种叫沸腾的现象，就是水向上渗入坟墓。
工人们说，近几个月来，他们已经处理了其中的一些问题，身体已经从邻近的墓地掉到了新的墓地。(细心的犹太人通常被埋在四分之一英寸的松木盒子里，这些盒子在地下很快就会腐烂。)。母板公司还看到了一张倒塌和被洪水淹没的坟墓的照片。这些工作条件对大流行来说并不新鲜，其中一些在所有墓地都会出现。但他们说，严重的人手不足，低工资，由于地质条件而不幸地亵渎身体，以及设备故障，让掘墓人处于崩溃的临界点。
“身体堆放得非常紧密，”一名掘墓人说，这呼应了向OSHA提出的投诉，OSHA声称在狭小的空间里从事危险的工作。
他们补充说，被体液溅到脸上的情况并不少见。一旦你挖到一定深度以下，身体的汁液就会开始填满坟墓。下面的人身上都是腐烂的肉。我们用干草盖住拆开的棺材，遮住人们的眼睛。由于活动量很大，现在和以前有很大的不同。“。
尽管大流行让他们的工作变得更加繁重-一名工人描述说，在4月份最糟糕的几周里，他们在凌晨3点从压力梦中惊醒-但掘墓人表示，他们在大流行期间面临的是这个168英亩公墓长期管理不善和成本削减的后果。贝丝以色列公司的工人描述了严重的人员不足，反应迟钝和敌对的管理，设备不足，他们说是未能在安全和薪酬问题上与工会合作，以及StoneMor未能提供家属支付的葬礼和墓地服务。
贝斯以色列的事态对许多埋葬在那里的人的家人来说是毁灭性的，也是令人愤怒的。这座公墓是许多纳粹集中营的幸存者以及来自南威廉斯堡和布鲁克林其他社区的东正教和哈西德派犹太会众的最后安息之地。贝丝以色列将自己标榜为田园绿洲--这是一片“宁静的公园般的土地，花圃林立，林荫大道”，具有“真正的宗教意义”。但母板审查了过去10年向新泽西公墓委员会提交的数十起投诉，指控该公墓未能提供家庭支付数千美元购买的服务，比如常绿植物种植、除草以及牌匾和墓碑维护。新泽西公墓委员会负责监管该州的公墓并为其发放许可证。
母板自己与工人、家人的谈话，以及我在7月下旬前往墓地的访问，证实了向墓地委员会提出的官方投诉中的一些指控。在我的旅行中，贴有关怀贴纸的坟墓(意思是这家人已经支付了护理费用)被推倒，上面长满了野草。在我的旅行中，贴着关怀标签的坟墓被掀翻了，上面长满了野草。
“我们严重缺人，”贝丝·伊斯雷尔的一名掘墓人在7月份的电话中告诉“母板”(Motherboard)。“由于人手不足，维修工作不能像我们以前做得那么好。这地方看起来很荒凉，但实际上很活跃。墓碑被掀翻了。你的杂草齐腰深。所有的内部街区都被忽略了。人们支付了数千美元，却得不到他们支付的东西。“
当被问及掘墓人是否会考虑将他的家人埋葬在贝斯以色列时，他说：“我想你知道答案。绝对不是。“。
当被反复问及本文中的具体指控时，StoneMor的一位发言人否认了所有指控。
“我们不同意电子邮件中提供的任何声明。在此期间，StoneMor Partners和贝丝以色列公墓一直在与我们所有的员工和家人勤奋工作，以确保我们为我们的家人提供安全的工作环境和有尊严的体验。“。“所提供的陈述是虚假和误导性的。我们很高兴与任何员工或客户直接交谈，这样我们就可以帮助他们解决他们的问题。“。
StoneMor Partners一再拒绝讨论任何具体指控。当拿到一系列照片时，例如，撞倒的墓碑和被洪水淹没的坟墓，StoneMor的一位发言人说，谢谢你伸出援手。我的声明是一样的。
在大流行期间，StoneMor的首席执行官、美国在线国际公司(AOL International)前首席执行官乔·雷德林(Joe Redling)同意放弃基本工资的一半，为期10周，相当于他2019年330万美元收入的2%。与此同时，贝丝以色列的工人们说，StoneMor拒绝向他们提供危险津贴，并花了几周的时间才提供眼睛保护和洗手液，尽管工人们正在处理假定的新冠肺炎遇难者的遗骸，这些遗骸没有按照犹太人的做法进行防腐处理。StoneMor在声明中否认了这一说法，但拒绝提供任何证据或信息，说明工人上一次加薪是在什么时候，他们是否获得了危险津贴，以及他们是在什么时候获得PPE的。
根据工人和卡车司机当地469的说法，在4月份他们的工会与管理层的合同谈判中，该公司声称没有必要保护眼睛，因为“COVID不能溅到他们的眼睛里”。(StoneMor分发的传单上写道，在流感大流行期间，掘墓人必须佩戴护目镜或面罩等防护眼镜。)。
虽然死亡护理行业仍然主要由夫妻店和墓地组成，但StoneMor和它的一些竞争对手已经推动了该行业的整合。分析人士估计，排名前六位的死亡护理公司控制着全国大约25%的葬礼服务。
2018年，雷德林被请来偿还StoneMor的债务，并使其盈利，但在他的领导下，该公司的股价暴跌了80%。自2015年以来，其股价已从30美元跌至80美分。去年，该公司聘请了一个咨询集团，为削减价值3000万美元的运营成本提供建议。
与此同时，StoneMor面临着来自客户、员工和州政府的定期法律行动。今年4月，它与宾夕法尼亚州总检察长乔希·夏皮罗(Josh Shapiro)达成和解，并同意向消费者和该州支付5万美元。夏皮罗写道，StoneMor“未能有意义地告知消费者，它是如何与预付葬礼或葬礼服务费用的客户签订协议的。”在和解协议中，StoneMor否认有任何过错或不当行为。
西弗吉尼亚州的一名男子在2019年提起集体诉讼，因为他说他父亲的坟墓和西弗吉尼亚州佩克米尔石碑公司拥有的墓地里的其他人遭受了墓碑下沉、墓碑损坏、挖掘、盗窃和瓦砾的影响。此案在审判前被驳回，因为该男子的母亲还活着；法院裁定，只有她才有资格提起诉讼。2020年7月，西弗吉尼亚州Kanawha的一个家庭起诉了StoneMor，据称在他们心爱的人的墓地里发现了一具神秘的身体，StoneMor称这是该墓地的前主人做的错葬。在伊利诺伊州，一个家庭起诉StoneMor双重出售他们儿子的墓地和移动他的身体。StoneMor的一位发言人告诉西北先驱报，该公司知道这一情况，并正在努力解决，但拒绝与主板讨论这一案件。
2015年，StoneMor在一起诉讼中同意向俄亥俄州的650名员工支付230万美元的和解费用，指控该公司没有支付加班费。根据Motherboard查阅的法庭记录，它在马里兰州、加利福尼亚州、科罗拉多州、堪萨斯州、密苏里州、俄勒冈州和华盛顿州面临-并支付了和解费用-类似的案件。StoneMor否认在这些案件中有过错。StoneMor拒绝对提交给主板的案件发表评论。
十年前，贝丝以色列公司雇佣了28名全职维修工。2004年，StoneMor首次上市，随后对死亡护理行业进行了整合，通过一系列裁员、解雇和未填补职位的员工离职，员工人数稳步缩减至不到这个数字的一半。OSHA和NLRB的投诉都详细说明了员工人数的减少。据Teamsters Local 469称，今天，18名全职墓地工人仍在工作，他们的任务是维护贝丝·以色列的8万个坟墓和附近较小的公墓三叶草。
工人们面临着“非常严重的健康和安全威胁，原因是人手不足，设施、车辆和设备维护不力，OSHA的申诉仍在审理中，”OSHA称。工人们非常担心(贝丝以色列)的健康和安全状况，他们正在考虑未来拒绝做这项工作，除非它是安全的。“。
母板还审查了过去10年向新泽西州公墓委员会提交的数十起投诉。
该委员会的一位发言人告诉Motherboard，针对贝丝·以色列公墓的投诉都没有导致纪律处分，也无法提供具体信息，说明与其他公墓相比，该公墓的投诉数量是否不成比例，并补充说，投诉数量根据“公墓的活动、规模和天气事件”而有所不同。公墓委员会表示，投诉通常在公墓和顾客之间得到解决，这意味着不会采取纪律行动，但这并不一定意味着投诉没有价值。
在回答一系列详细的问题和具体指控时，StoneMor的一名发言人表示，他们不能直接对“个别家庭事务”发表评论，但认为投诉中的指控，以及本文中的每一项指控，都是“严重误导和不真实的”。当Motherboard要求对具体指控发表评论时，该公司拒绝在随后的交易所中详细说明这一声明。
在其中一份投诉中，德克萨斯州的客户桑福德·菲特林(Sanford Fitlin)在2019年8月给董事会写道：“我的父母葬在这个公墓里，我震惊地发现，大约三分之一的墓地的墓碑已经倒塌或摇摇欲坠。水泥人行道弯曲了，墓床放错了地方，石凳被弄坏了，到处都是垃圾……但他们还在继续卖地皮。钱都到哪里去了？“。
母板与菲特林通了电话，菲特林说，在他提出申诉后，墓地告诉他“我们无能为力”。
“石头被推翻了，”他继续说。“人行道都裂开了。我对这个问题还有点恼火。如果可以的话，我会把我父母的遗体从墓地移走。“。
在另一份诉状中，鳏夫斯蒂芬·布拉特(Stephen Bratter)说，他曾在2015年向墓地投诉他妻子的坟墓被忽视了，他为此购买了捐赠护理，并“得到了问题会得到纠正的保证”。但在2017年参观墓地时，他心烦意乱地发现“该地区的条件完全相同”。在向公墓委员会提出正式投诉后，布拉特告诉母板，他再次得到保证，这些问题已经得到解决，但由于健康问题，他没有回到公墓确认。
“人们带着破碎的心去墓地，”布拉特在电话中告诉“母板”(Motherboard)。“他们不想看到埋在地下的牌匾，满是洞的坟墓。”
母板审查了2018年的两份文件，贝丝以色列公司的管理层对服务报告进行了统计和分类，这两份报告是维护墓地所需完成的任务。这些东西包括修剪树篱、除草和重新播种草坪，但也包括像下沉的坟墓(当时是24个)、拉直纪念碑等少数几个类别。截至2018年4月，公墓主动服务举报510件，截至2018年6月，增至710件。工人们说，他们大部分时间都在挖坟墓，跟不上越来越多的服务报告。
7月下旬，在一个酷热的日子里，我拜访了贝丝·伊斯雷尔(Beth Israel)，我花了一个多小时才走过了墓地的一小部分，那里俯瞰着花园州立公园大道(Garden State Parkway)和一系列写字楼和共管公寓。面对道路的墓碑看起来整齐整齐，鲜花盛开，美国国旗挥舞，墓碑竖立。
但走进墓碑的海洋深处，在犹太教会和社团组织的许多地区，我看到数十块墓碑被推倒，倾斜或劈成两半，枯死的植物，杂草丛生的灌木，以及破裂的人行道。许多倒塌的墓碑上都贴着“捐赠”的贴纸，这意味着这家人已经为五年或一生的护理支付了数千美元。主板向该公司发送了这一点的照片证据；该公司拒绝置评。
(据贝丝以色列公司的一名高级员工透露，贝斯以色列公司五年的捐赠护理目前为家庭支付了4990美元的种植坟墓费用，1900美元的鹅卵石坟墓费用。)。
去年3月，贝丝·以色列(Beth以色列)和邻近的StoneMor拥有的公墓CloverLeaf的挖墓工投票决定与卡车司机Local 469成立工会，加入了美国StoneMor拥有的至少另外19个成立工会的公墓。工人们希望在工会的支持下，抵制人手不足、设备破旧和工资停滞不前的局面。根据工会的说法，贝丝·以色列的工会工人，包括一些时薪13.50美元的工人，三年来没有一人获得加薪。StoneMor拒绝提供任何具体的证据或文件来驳斥这一点。
根据Local 469向NLRB提交的一份起诉书，当月晚些时候，该公司解雇了7名工会成员，并解雇了来自贝丝以色列(Beth Israel)和StoneMor拥有的两个邻近墓地的一名亲工会负责人，将剩下的挖墓人分散到了更薄的地方。
在工会运动期间和之后，StoneMor向工人分发反工会传单，并向他们的家中邮寄信件，反复敦促他们在墓地公墓的公告栏上投反对票，宣传单上的标题是当地469年赢了什么，管理层写道，你不能信任当地469人。它的承诺是空洞的。投反对票！留着你的钱。在工会选举后分发给工人的另一份传单中，母板获得了标题为StoneMor2.0的经理们写道，StoneMor有一个全新的结构和一个全新的领导者团队…。不要相信工会的陌生人。
自选举以来，工会代表工人向全国劳资关系委员会(NLRB)提出了两项不公平劳动行为指控，指控StoneMor拒绝通过解雇新工会成员来真诚讨价还价，雇佣分包商，以及与工会作战。根据Motherboard审阅的一份NLRB和解协议副本，今年4月，StoneMor同意就一系列指控达成和解，在贝丝·以色列和立体式建筑的7名下岗工人中平分5万美元。分发给主板的副本没有StoneMor的签名，但工人们说他们收到了付款。
工会尚未批准其第一份合同，并在5月份向全国劳资关系委员会(NLRB)提出了另一项不公平的劳工行为指控，此前StoneMor聘请了承包商-受雇于一家名为Moon的景观公司的中美洲移民工人，他们是从宾夕法尼亚州乘坐大巴从宾夕法尼亚州赶来的-在疫情最严重的时候协助手工挖掘坟墓。NLRB的起诉书称，2020年4月3日，StoneMor给[工会]写了一封电子邮件，声明公司不打算转包谈判单位的工作。三周后，StoneMor开始非法转包谈判单位的工作。StoneMor拒绝对投诉的细节发表评论。
在OSHA的一份投诉中，贝丝以色列公司的工人声称墓地的机器已经非常过时了。根据起诉书，其中包括石油泄漏的照片，生锈和严重凹陷。

note_en:
In April, 13 gravediggers at Beth Israel, a Jewish cemetery in a working class New Jersey township across the street from one of the state’s largest malls, buried roughly 300 bodies—many of them presumed by workers to be victims of COVID-19.
  In a typical month, the crew at Beth Israel Cemetery digs between 50 and 100 graves. During the peak of the coronavirus outbreak in New Jersey and neighboring New York City, where the virus tore through many  Hasidic Jewish neighborhoods with ties to Beth Israel, the gravediggers took to the fields at the earliest hours of daylight to pour foundation, lay out wood, dig trenches, set up funerals, carry caskets, and fill graves with backhoes. Gravediggers say they often worked ten-and-a-half-hour shifts, and have not gotten hazard pay and only sporadic bathroom and lunch breaks, according to  Teamsters Local 469, the gravediggers' union.
  “I’d never seen anything like it in my life. We did 10 to 14 burials every day for six weeks,” a gravedigger who works at Beth Israel told Motherboard. A burial schedule obtained by Motherboard shows a day in April, for example, in which 10 burials and 5 mausoleum entombments were scheduled in a four-and-a-half-hour period. Workers say it was at times impossible to complete all the burials in the allotted time.
  “We had such a high volume of work and so few workers that we had to leave people’s loved ones in graves left open for four to seven hours, and even overnight, going against Jewish tradition,” he continued. “I don’t think families would have appreciated it much to learn that that happened.”
  Motherboard granted anonymity to five current Beth Israel gravediggers to allow them to speak candidly about their working conditions. The gravediggers fear retaliation from their employer, a company called StoneMor corporation, for speaking to the press. StoneMor is the second-largest cemetery and funeral home corporation in the United States, overseeing 321 cemeteries and 90 funeral homes in 27 states and Puerto Rico.
  The gravediggers at Beth Israel are unionized with  Teamsters Local 469, but have yet to sign a collective bargaining contract, and have in recent months filed two unfair labor practice charges with the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) and a 56-page Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) complaint against StoneMor. According to NLRB complaints reviewed by Motherboard, the workers have had some of their work subcontracted out to workers who earn even less, which they say is illegal.
  According to Teamsters Local 469, Beth Israel's lowest paid gravediggers, many who are Latinx, earn less than $15 an hour. Less than a mile down the road, at Mount Lebanon Cemetery, where workers unionized more than 30 years ago, gravediggers make at least $24.62 an hour, according to the union official. Gravediggers at Beth Israel are well aware of the pay disparity.
  “I work Monday through Friday and every other Sunday, and I do handiwork on the side, and it’s still not enough to live on. I got four small kids,” a gravedigger told Motherboard. “I came to this job because of the union. We thought we could get a big raise, but we’re still waiting to see what’s going on.”
  Despite this pay disparity relative to a nearby cemetery, workers at Beth Israel still have to deal with what, under the best of circumstances, is grim work. StoneMor presentation slides and information sheets obtained by Motherboard explain that workers "may come into contact with human body fluids or tissue" from people who may have died of Covid-19, and explains that grave cave-ins can happen and that "the most common cause of worker fatalities is excavations." The documentation also explains that there are specific geological conditions at Beth Israel that can make burials challenging, such as a high water table that leaves graves subject to a phenomenon called "boiling," where water seeps upward into a grave.
  Workers say that they have dealt with some of these problems in recent months, and that corpses have fallen from neighboring grave sites into new ones. (Observant Jews are often buried in quarter-inch pine boxes that decompose quickly in the ground.) Motherboard has also viewed a photo of a collapsed and flooded grave. These working conditions are not new to the pandemic, and some of them arise in all graveyards. But the severe understaffing, low wages, unfortunate desecration of bodies because of geological conditions, and out-of-order equipment, have gravediggers at a breaking point, they say.
  “Bodies are packed very close together,” one gravedigger said, echoing a complaint made to OSHA which alleges dangerous work in "tight spaces."
    "It’s not uncommon to get splashed in the face with bodily fluids," they added. "Once you dig below a certain depth, body juice starts filling up the grave. Guys are down there with rotting flesh. We use hay to cover disassembled caskets, shield people’s eyes from that sight. It’s a lot different than it used to be because of the volume of activity.”
    Although the pandemic made their work more taxing—one worker described jolting awake from stress dreams at 3 AM during the worst weeks in April—the gravediggers say that what they have faced during the pandemic is the consequence of long-standing mismanagement and cost-cutting at the 168-acre cemetery. Beth Israel workers describe severe understaffing, unresponsive and antagonistic management, inadequate equipment, what they say is a failure to work with the union on safety and pay issues, and a failure on the part of StoneMor to provide burial and graveside services families had paid for.
  The state of affairs at Beth Israel has been devastating and infuriating for many families of those buried there. The cemetery is a final resting place for many survivors of Nazi concentration camps and members of Orthodox and Hasidic Jewish congregations from South Williamsburg and other Brooklyn neighborhoods. Beth Israel  advertises itself as a pastoral oasis—a  “serene park-like land with flowering garden beds and tree lined avenues” with “true religious significance.” But Motherboard reviewed dozens of complaints from the past 10 years filed with the New Jersey Cemetery Board, which regulates and licenses cemeteries in the state, alleging that the cemetery has failed to provide services that families have paid thousands of dollars for, such as evergreen plantings, weed-pulling, and plaque and tombstone maintenance.
  Motherboard’s own conversations with workers, families, and my visit to the cemetery in late July, confirmed some of the allegations from the official complaints made with the cemetery board. On my trip, graves with "endowed care" stickers (meaning the family had paid for care) were toppled and covered with weeds.
  “We’re severely unstaffed,” a Beth Israel gravedigger told Motherboard on the phone in July. “The upkeep can’t be done as well as we used to because of the lack of manpower. This place looks abandoned but it’s actually active. Gravestones are tipped over. You’re up to your waist in weeds. All of the interior blocks are neglected. People are paying thousands of dollars and not getting what they paid for.”
  Asked if the gravedigger would consider burying his family members at Beth Israel, he said, “I think you know the answer to that. Absolutely not.”
  When asked repeatedly about the specific allegations in this article, a StoneMor spokesperson denied all of the claims.
  “We do not agree with any of the statements that are provided in the email. StoneMor Partners and Beth Israel Cemetery have been diligently working with all of our employees and families during this time to assure that we have provided safe working environments and a dignified experience for our families,” she said. “The statements provided are false and misleading. We are happy to speak with any employee or customer directly so we may assist them with their concern.”
  StoneMor Partners repeatedly declined to discuss any specific allegations. When provided with a series of photos that showed, for example, knocked over headstones and a flooded grave, a spokesperson for StoneMor said "thank you for reaching out. My statement remains the same."
   During the pandemic, Joe Redling, StoneMor’s CEO—and former CEO of AOL International— agreed to give up half of his base salary for 10 weeks, amounting to two percent of the  $3.3 million he earned in 2019. Meanwhile, Beth Israel workers say StoneMor refused to provide them hazard pay, and took weeks to offer eye protection and hand sanitizer even though workers were handling the remains of presumed COVID-19 victims that had not been embalmed, as dictated by Jewish practice. StoneMor denied the claim in its statement but declined to provide any proof or information about when workers last got a raise, whether they got hazard pay, and when they got PPE.
  In their union’s contract negotiations with management in April, the company claimed that eye protection was not necessary because “COVID could not splash in their eyes,” according to workers and the Teamsters Local 469. (A flyer distributed by StoneMor states that "protective eyewear such as goggles or face shield" must be worn by gravediggers during the pandemic.)
  While the death care industry  still consists primarily of mom-and-pop-owned funeral parlors and cemeteries, StoneMor and a few of its competitors have pushed to consolidate the industry. Analysts estimate that the top six death care companies control roughly 25 percent of the funeral services in the country.
  In 2018, Redling was brought in to pay down StoneMor’s debts and turn it profitable, but under his watch, the company’s stock price has tumbled 80 percent. Since 2015, its share price  has fallen from $30 to 80 cents. Last year, the company  hired a consulting group to advise on cutting $30 million worth of operational costs.
  Meanwhile, StoneMor has faced regular legal action from customers, employees, and state governments. In April,  it settled with Pennsylvania’s attorney general Josh Shapiro, and agreed to pay $50,000 to the consumers and the state. Shapiro wrote StoneMor had “failed to meaningfully inform consumers" about how it operated its agreements with customers who pre-pay for burial or funeral services. In the settlement, StoneMor denied any fault or wrongdoing.
  A West Virginia man filed a  class action lawsuit in 2019 because he said his father's grave and others at a StoneMor owned cemetery in Peck's Mill, West Virginia had been "subjected to sinking headstones, damaged headstones, excavation, theft, and debris." The case was thrown out pretrial because the man's mother was still alive; the court ruled that only she would have standing to sue. In July 2020, a family in Kanawha, West Virginia  sued StoneMor after a mysterious body was allegedly found in their loved one's gravesite; StoneMor said that it was a "misburial" done by a previous owner of that graveyard. In Illinois, a family sued  StoneMor for double-selling their son's gravesite and moving his body. A StoneMor spokesperson told the  Northwest Herald that the company is aware of the situation and working to resolve it, but declined to discuss this case with Motherboard.
  In 2015, StoneMor agreed to pay a $2.3 million settlement to 650 employees in Ohio in a lawsuit that alleged the company did not pay overtime. It faced—and paid settlements in—similar cases in Maryland, California, Colorado, Kansas, Missouri, Oregon, and Washington, according to court records reviewed by Motherboard. StoneMor denied fault in those cases. StoneMor declined to comment on the cases to Motherboard.
  A decade ago, Beth Israel employed 28 full-time maintenance workers. Following StoneMor’s 2004 debut on the stock market, and its subsequent  consolidation of the death care industry, the staff steadily shrunk to less than half that size through a series of layoffs, firings, and the departures of workers whose positions weren’t filled. The declining staff size is detailed in both OSHA and NLRB complaints. Today, 18 full-time graveworkers, who are tasked with the upkeep of Beth Israel’s 80,000 graves and the smaller neighboring cemetery Cloverleaf, remain, according to Teamsters Local 469.
  Workers face “very serious health and safety hazards due to understaffing and failure to maintain facilities, vehicles, and equipment," the OSHA complaint, which is still open, states. "Workers are greatly concerned about health and safety conditions at [Beth Israel] and are considering refusing to do this work in the future unless it is made safe.”
    Motherboard also reviewed dozens of complaints filed over the past 10 years with the New Jersey Cemetery Board.
  A spokesperson for the board told Motherboard that none of the complaints filed against Beth Israel Cemetery have resulted in disciplinary action, and could not offer specific information on whether the cemetery has a disproportionate number of complaints compared to other cemeteries, adding that the numbers vary based on the “cemetery’s activity, size, and weather events.” The cemetery board said that complaints are often resolved between the cemetery and the customer, which means no disciplinary action takes place, but doesn't necessarily mean a complaint doesn't have merit.
    In response to a detailed list of questions and specific allegations, a StoneMor spokesperson said that they could not directly comment on “individual family matters” but believed the allegations in the complaints, as well as every allegation in this article, to be “grossly misleading and untrue.” The company declined to elaborate on this statement in subsequent exchanges when Motherboard asked for comment on specific allegations.
  In one of the complaints, Sanford Fitlin, a Texas-based customer, wrote to the Board in August 2019, “My parents are buried at this cemetery, I was appalled to observe that about one third of the gravesites have headstones that have fallen over or are leaning precariously. Cement walkways are buckled, graves beds are out of place, stone benches are broken and littered about...Yet they continue to sell plots. Where does the money go?”
  Motherboard spoke to Fitlin on the phone, who said that the cemetery told him “there’s nothing we can do,” after he filed his complaint.
  “Stones were toppled,” he continued. “Walkways were cracked. I’m still a little irate over the issue. If I could, I would remove my parents’ remains from the cemetery.”
  In another complaint, a widower, Stephen Bratter, said he complained to the cemetery in 2015 about the neglect of his wife’s grave, for which he had purchased endowed care, and “was assured the issues would be rectified.” But upon visiting the cemetery in 2017, he was upset to find “the area in the exact same conditions.” After lodging a formal complaint with the Cemetery Board, Bratter told Motherboard he had been assured yet again that the issues had been addressed, but due to health problems, he has not returned to the cemetery to confirm.
  “People visit cemeteries with a broken heart,” Bratter told Motherboard on the phone. “They don’t want to see plaques that are sunk into the ground, graves full of holes.”
  Motherboard reviewed two documents from 2018 where Beth Israel management tallied and categorized "service reports," which are tasks that needed to be done to maintain the cemetery. These include things like trimming hedges, weeding, and reseeding lawns, but also include things like "sunken grave" (24 at the time), "straighten monument" and a handful of other categories. As of April 2018, the cemetery had 510 active service reports.  By June 2018, the number had increased to 710. Workers say that they spend most of their time digging graves and cannot keep up with the mounting number of service reports.
  When I visited Beth Israel on a baking hot day in late July, it took more than an hour to traverse just a small fraction of the cemetery that overlooks the Garden State Parkway and a series of office towers and condos. The tombstones that faced the road appeared well-groomed with fresh flowers, American flags waving, and tombstones erect.
    But walking deeper into the sea of headstones, in many areas organized by Jewish congregations and societies, I saw dozens of headstones knocked over and tilted or split in half, dead plantings, overgrown shrubs, and cracking sidewalks. Many of the toppled headstones are marked with “endowed” stickers, meaning the family had paid thousands of dollars for either five years or a lifetime of care. Motherboard sent photo evidence of this to the company; it declined to comment.
  (Five years of endowed care at Beth Israel currently costs families $4,990 for graves with plantings, and $1,900 for graves with pebbles, according to a senior Beth Israel employee.)
   Last March, gravediggers at Beth Israel and the neighboring StoneMor-owned cemetery Cloverleaf voted to unionize with Teamsters Local 469, joining  at least 19 other unionized cemeteries owned by StoneMor in the United States. Workers wanted to push back against understaffing, dilapidated equipment, and stagnant wages with the power of a union behind them. None of Beth Israel’s unionized workers, including some who earn $13.50 an hour, have gotten a raise in three years, according to the union. StoneMor declined to provide any specific proof or documentation that would refute this.
  Later that month, the company laid off seven union members and fired a pro-union superintendent from Beth Israel and two neighboring cemeteries owned by StoneMor, spreading the remaining grave diggers even thinner, according to a complaint filed by Local 469 with the NLRB.
  During and after the union drive, StoneMor distributed anti-union flyers to workers and mailed letters to their homes, repeatedly urging them to "vote no." In a flyer posted on a bulletin at the cemetery titled "What Local 469 Won't Tell You," management wrote, "You Can't Trust Local 469. Its Promises are Empty. Vote No! Keep Your Money." In another  flyer distributed to workers after the union election and obtained by Motherboard with the title "StoneMor 2.0," managers wrote, "StoneMor has a whole new structure and a whole new team of leaders… Don't trust a union stranger."
  Since the election, the union has filed two unfair labor practice charges on behalf of the workers with the NLRB against StoneMor for refusing to bargain in good faith by terminating new union members, for hiring subcontractors, and for fighting against the union. And in April, StoneMor agreed to settle a set of charges, splitting $50,000 among seven laid off workers from Beth Israel and Cloverleaf, according to a copy of the NLRB settlement agreement that was reviewed by Motherboard. The copy distributed to Motherboard was not signed by StoneMor, but workers say they received their payments.
  The union has yet to ratify its first contract, and filed another unfair labor practice charge with the NLRB in May after StoneMor hired contractors—Central American migrant workers employed by a landscaping company called Moon who are bussed in from Pennsylvania—to assist in hand-digging graves during the worst of the pandemic. "On April 3, 2020, StoneMor wrote an email to [the union] stating that the company did not plan to subcontract bargaining unit work," the NLRB complaint says. Three weeks later, StoneMor began to "illegally subcontract bargaining unit work," it says. StoneMor declined to comment on the specifics of the complaint.
   In an OSHA complaint, Beth Israel workers allege that the cemetery's machinery is woefully out of date. According to the complaint, which included photos of oil leaks, rusted and heavily dented

一个女孩独自坐在一张丑陋的沙发上，抚摸着一条塑料鱼，嘴里念着杰森·德鲁洛(Jason Derulo)的“独自骑行”(Ridin‘Solo)。她的眼睛充血。她戴上墨镜把它们遮住，就像德鲁洛在歌中说的那样。
这段视频于7月份上传到TikTok，点击量超过1140万次。这是由@mooptopia发布的，他在7月初不知从哪里冒出来，后来成为了一颗意想不到的明星。她现在有250万粉丝，尽管她的账号没有什么值得注意的：她是一个十几岁的孩子，住在似乎是一个普通的郊区，在那里她走来走去，笨拙地跳舞，有时还会咆哮。
这一集最引人注目的是它的典型性。在TikTok上成名的速度非常快。在几个月的时间里，一个精力充沛的青少年可以建立起一批观众，这可以转化为数百万美元的代言交易；一个无聊的怪胎可以扮成Waluigi跳“WAP”舞，吸引了超过700万的浏览量，开启了一种荒谬的新趋势；一个女孩可以第一口喝上一口康普茶，感觉非常矛盾，成为“康普茶女孩”。年轻人也会在YouTube和Instagram等社交媒体平台上走红，但TikTok的独特之处在于它的算法是如何从蓝色中提取古怪的东西，并将它们推入数百万人看到的主要订阅源。而这数百万人中的许多人都是青少年。超过40%的TikTok用户年龄在16岁到24岁之间，联邦贸易委员会(Federal Trade Commission)声称，“相当大”一部分用户甚至比这一年龄更年轻。布鲁克大学(Brock University)研究TikTok的教授肖娜·波梅兰茨(Shauna Pmerantz)说，现在，由于这场流行病，他们都比以前花了更多的时间在这款应用上，这似乎是不可能的。
TikTok上的女孩比男孩多得多，而且大多数走红的青少年似乎都是女孩。波美兰茨告诉我：“TikTok非常专注于Goofiness，而所有其他平台都不是这样。”“这对女孩子来说很有趣。在你可以逃脱惩罚的事情上有更多的自由。“。女孩们有崇拜其他女孩的倾向，这导致所有的花花公子都出现在像Mooptopia这样的荒谬视频中。但走红可能会带来离奇的后果和新的压力。当青少年突然受到名声不那么有趣的一面的打击时，对他们和他们的父母来说，这可能是可怕的，也是令人困惑的，因为他们可能根本不知道自己的孩子在做什么。
没有太多关于Mooptopia的信息表明她会成为TikTok上的病毒式明星，但她和你们高中最受欢迎的女孩一样受欢迎，成百上千倍的人气。这只是她的几个视频：她在车道上漫步，拿着一根大棍子，按照爵士乐的标准摇摆；她在一双破旧的Birkenstock鞋的鞋底钉钉子，小心翼翼地四处走动，配音是碧昂斯(Beyoncé)混编的梅根·特·斯塔莱恩(Megan Thee Stallion)的“野性”(Savage)。她最常见的动作是四肢着地冲到镜头前，就像一个着魔的蹒跚学步的孩子。这可能是她迄今为止最巧妙的作品，她将拉娜·德雷(Lana Del Rey)的“夏天的悲伤”(Summertime Sad)与她浴室的慢锅配对，然后是红葡萄落入马桶的特写镜头，一个接一个。(Mooptopia没有回应多次采访请求。)。
当我向英国莱斯特大学(University Of Leicester)的媒体研究员梅勒妮·肯尼迪(Melanie Kennedy)询问TikTok对这样的视频的需求时，她强调了平台上“女孩文化”的受欢迎程度。肯尼迪认为，许多女孩在短短几天内从没有粉丝到数十万，部分原因是TikTok将她们几十年来一直在做的事情-在卧室里跳舞，与朋友开玩笑，与父母打架-“变成了一场全球奇观”。来自TikTok的每一种主要文化趋势都是一种女孩文化趋势：VSCO女孩，e-Girls，由女孩创作并被其他女孩复制的舞蹈。从数字上看，16岁的查利·达梅里奥(Charli D‘Amelio)在TikTok上的知名度和蕾哈娜在Instagram上的知名度差不多，比总统在Twitter上的知名度还要高。达梅里奥在该平台上掀起了许多最大的舞蹈狂潮。
一些青少年变得如此出名，以至于他们现在都有专门的粉丝，就像流行歌星或政客一样。20岁的费斯·霍尔(Faith Hall)来自华盛顿，在Tumblr上经营着一个Mooptopia粉丝页面，由于这个神秘的故事，她被Moop吸引了-她的粉丝将Moop的用户名缩写为Moop。与许多其他TikTok明星不同，她在应用程序上根本不使用自己的真名。当她的账户在7月份起飞时，有关于Moop的阴谋论，因为她似乎毫不费力就变得如此之大。她甚至没有在她的视频上使用标签，这是让他们获得更多眼球的最常见和最明显的策略。
霍尔记得她看到的第一个Moop视频：Mooptopia在停车场里，嘴里唱着一首歌的歌词，做着奇怪的动作。“她什么也没做，真的，”霍尔告诉我。当我问霍尔“Mooptopia”的粉丝们看上她什么的时候，
现在，数十个Instagram账号都致力于热爱Mooptopia。管理账户的主要是年轻女孩-我发的第一条信息是14岁-很崇拜她。他们制作扇子艺术。他们在转售应用DePOP上为她购买二手衣服，以及她出售的T恤和贴纸。他们保留了她每次喜欢或评论其内容的历史记录。他们在TikTok上绘制了她的成长图表，在她达到重要里程碑时向她表示祝贺。
一小群TikTok巨星已经成为真正的名人：达梅里奥在一年内积累了8600万粉丝，她在Dunkin‘有自己的限时饮品。但是，几乎任何在这个平台上取得成功的人都会有粉丝账户网络。男孩也在网上走红，但他们的粉丝似乎不成比例地是女孩：19岁的杜克·德普(Duke Depp)的粉丝页面弹出了大量页面，就在今年春天，他开始装扮成约翰尼·德普(Johnny Depp)的化身威利·旺卡(Willy Wonka)，自那以来已经获得了340万粉丝。我随机点击了一个账号，看到了一张模糊的照片，照片上是一个十几岁的女孩，她在米色的卧室里，脸上覆盖着分层的文字，
早在TikTok出现之前，青少年就像病毒一样传播开来：女孩经常被要求提供一种文化的轻浮，而年轻女孩总是尊敬年龄较大的女孩，并试图效仿她们。在大萧条之后，数百万人在雪莉·坦普尔的踢踏舞中找到了希望；娱乐业帮助布兰妮·斯皮尔斯成为年轻女孩崇拜的对象。但是，TikTok上出现这种名声的速度伴随着各种各样的新考虑。
这种投入和无休止的关注本身可能很难抗衡，特别是对青少年来说。他们被当作媒体产品对待，也被当做媒体产品对待，而没有像真正的名人那样做好准备。“Mooptopia”在上映后的最初几周内就遭遇了名气的压力。一张她站在唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)牌子前的旧照片让这群人指责她秘密地忠于玛加；她庞大的追随者天生的好奇心导致业余侦探挖掘出她的真名和她高中的位置；她出现在一个致力于年轻TikTokers脚下的变态副编辑网站上。粉丝们并不总是喜欢她在视频中采取的方向。当她尝试稍微有些典型的TikTok舞蹈套路和迷人的镜头时，他们抱怨道：“Moop正在慢慢恢复正常。”在她出人意料地成为明星仅几周后，似乎是为了回应这一切，她宣布结束，发布了一个黑屏，上面覆盖了部分文字，上面写道，“最近对我来说很艰难，我必须离开TikTok。”
Mooptopia在退出TikTok几天后就卷土重来。“不要再离开我们了，女王，”她的粉丝恳求道。一些粉丝已经为窥探道歉，包括Instagram账户的所有者，该账户专门展示她童年时拍摄的“罕见”摩普照片。他们用哭猫的表情符号写道：“我想我不可能成为一名侦探。”“对不起，女王。”这种忠诚的真诚给我留下了深刻的印象-这是我十几岁时为男孩乐队成员和迪士尼频道演员保留的东西。
对于父母来说，有一些明显的理由让他们害怕青少年可能在几天内在他们不完全理解的社交媒体应用上积累的权力和公众关注。观众可能反复无常、要求苛刻、刻薄；成年人可能令人毛骨悚然、危险；青少年可能缺乏远见-他们可能不想因为未成年人在网上说的话而永远为人所知。以15岁的克劳迪娅·康韦(Claudia Conway)为例，她因为在TikTok上发帖表达了她对特朗普的厌恶和对父母的愤怒，尤其是她的母亲、前总统高级顾问凯莉安·康韦(Kellyanne Conway)，而一夜成名。通常会在餐桌上发生的那种家庭口角，却被数百万人效仿。克劳迪娅多次发帖称需要离开马戏团休息一下，专注于自己的心理健康，她的账户爆出后不久，她的父母都辞去了工作。(乔治·康韦(George Conway)和凯莉安·康韦(Kellyanne Conway)拒绝对这篇报道的记录发表评论；克劳迪娅没有回复记者的置评请求。)。其他TikTok明星也谈到了成名后被忽悠或骚扰。
最重要的是，家长们可能会担心这样一个事实，即对女孩的奉承很少是公平的：正如肯尼迪在最近的一篇论文中指出的那样，TikTok上许多最受关注的女孩都是年轻、白人和富有的女孩。年轻的黑人创造者在这款应用上抗议他们受到的待遇已经有一段时间了。正如肯尼迪所说，这个平台“让一些女孩的童年变得过于显眼，而另一些则隐藏在她们的阴影中。”
但是，能让TikTok让父母如此恐惧的，正是它对孩子们如此有吸引力的原因。“TikTok是一种通用语。这是女孩们的通用语言，“波美兰茨说。“这也是个密码，因为成年人通常不会
TikTok是一个庞大的女孩网络，主要是相互交谈，根据大多数现有证据，她们知道自己在做什么。Mooptopia有足够的追随者开始考虑代言和平台外的成功，但出于只有她知道的原因，它故意开辟了一条截然不同的道路。TikTok的创建者团队表示，她没有与任何内部增长策略师合作，以建立更大的职业生涯。她可能获得了病毒式的名声，她可能也感受到了刺痛，但她的故事仍然是一个谜，就像她似乎喜欢的那样。

note_en:
A girl sits alone on an ugly couch, stroking a plastic fish and mouthing the words to Jason Derulo’s “Ridin’ Solo.” Her eyes are bloodshot. She puts on sunglasses to cover them up, just as Derulo says he does in the song.
  This  video, uploaded to TikTok in July, has more than 11.4 million views. It was posted by @mooptopia, who emerged from nowhere at the beginning of July and has since become an unexpected star. She now has 2.5 million followers, despite the fact that nothing about her account is remarkable: She’s a teenager in what seems to be an average suburb, where she walks around and dances awkwardly and sometimes growls.
  The most striking thing about this episode is how typical it is. Getting famous on TikTok can happen very quickly. In a matter of months, a highly driven teen can build an audience that translates into  millions of dollars in endorsement deals; a bored weirdo can  dress up as Waluigi and do the  “WAP” dance, attracting more than 7 million views and starting a ridiculous  new trend; a girl can take her first sip of kombucha and feel dramatically conflicted, becoming “ Kombucha Girl.” Young people go viral on social-media platforms such as YouTube and Instagram too, but TikTok is unique in how its algorithm pulls oddities out of the blue and pushes them into a  main feed seen by millions of people. And many of those millions of people are teens. More than  40 percent of TikTok users are ages 16 to 24, and the Federal Trade Commission has  alleged that a “significant” portion of users are even younger than that. Now, because of the pandemic—says Shauna Pomerantz, a Brock University professor who has studied TikTok—they’re all spending more time on the app than they did before, which hadn’t even seemed possible.
    Far  more girls are on TikTok than boys, and most of the teens achieving viral fame seem to be girls. “TikTok is so focused on goofiness, and all of the other platforms aren’t,” Pomerantz told me. “That’s interesting for girls. There’s a lot more freedom around what you can get away with.” Girls have an inclination to worship other girls, which has led entire fandoms to pop up around even nonsensical videos such as Mooptopia’s. But going viral can set up the potential for bizarre consequences and new pressures. When teens are suddenly hit with the less fun side of fame, it can be scary and confusing for both them and their parents, who might not know what their kids are up to at all.
   Not much about Mooptopia suggests that she’d be a viral TikTok star, but she’s as popular as the  most popular girl in your high school, multiplied by a hundred thousand. Just a few of her videos: She  strolls around a driveway, swaying to a jazz standard while holding a large stick; she works nails through the soles of a beat-up pair of Birkenstocks and  walks around gingerly, soundtracked by Beyoncé’s remix of Megan Thee Stallion’s “Savage.” Her most common  move is scuttling toward the camera on all fours, like a possessed toddler. In what’s perhaps her most  artful work so far, she pairs Lana Del Rey’s “Summertime Sadness” with a slow pan of her bathroom, then a close-up of red grapes dropping into the toilet, one by one. (Mooptopia did not respond to several requests for an interview.)
   When I asked Melanie Kennedy, a media researcher at the University of Leicester, in England, about the demand for TikTok for videos like this, she emphasized the popularity of “girl culture” on the platform. Many girls go from no followers to hundreds of thousands in a matter of days, in part because TikTok turns the kind of things they’ve been doing for decades—dancing in their bedroom, joking around with their friends, fighting with their parents—“into a global spectacle,” Kennedy argued. Every major cultural trend that has come from TikTok is a girl-culture trend: VSCO girls, e-girls, the dances created by girls and copied by other girls. By the numbers,  Charli D’Amelio, a 16-year-old who has popularized many of the platform’s biggest dance crazes, is about as famous on TikTok as Rihanna is on Instagram, and more famous than the president is on Twitter.
 Some teenagers have gotten so famous that they now have devoted fandoms, much the same as pop stars or politicians. Faith Hall, a 20-year-old from Washington who runs a Mooptopia  fan page on Tumblr, was drawn to Moop—whose username is abbreviated as such by her fans, because of the mystery. Unlike lots of other TikTok stars, she doesn’t use her real name at all on the app. When her account took off in July, there were  conspiracy theories about Moop, because she got so big with seemingly zero effort. She wasn’t even using hashtags on her videos, the most common and obvious tactic for getting them more eyeballs.
    Hall remembers the first Moop video she saw: Mooptopia was in a parking lot, mouthing the lyrics to a song and doing strange moves. “She’s not doing anything, really,” Hall told me. When I asked Hall what Mooptopia’s fans see in her, she said a lot of outsiders were just taking the whole thing too seriously. “I think, honestly, it started as a joke between her and her friends, and then it started getting big because of TikTok’s algorithm,” she said. “I truly think people liked her just because she was doing her own thing and having fun.”
 Now dozens of Instagram accounts are dedicated to loving Mooptopia. The predominantly young girls who run the accounts—the first one I messaged was 14— adore her. They make  fan  art. They buy her used clothes on the resale app Depop, as well as the T-shirts and stickers she sells. They  keep a history of each time she likes or comments on their content. They  chart her growth on TikTok, congratulating her when she hits important milestones.
 A small group of TikTok megastars have become actual celebrities: D’Amelio, who has accumulated 86 million followers in one year, has her own limited-time drink at Dunkin’. But networks of  fan  accounts exist for pretty much anyone who has made it big on the platform. Boys go viral too, but their fans appear disproportionately to be girls: A wealth of  fan  pages popped up for a 19-year-old named Duke Depp, who just this spring  started dressing up as Johnny Depp’s incarnation of Willy Wonka and has since gained 3.4 million fans. I clicked on  one account at random and saw a blurry up-the-chin photo of a preteen girl in a beige bedroom who had layered text over her own face,
   Teens were going viral long before TikTok even existed: Girls have often been tasked with providing a culture’s levity, and younger girls have always looked up to older girls and tried to emulate them. In the aftermath of the Great Depression, millions found hope in the tap dances of Shirley Temple; the entertainment industry helped turn Britney Spears into an object for young girls to worship. But the speed with which this kind of fame can happen on TikTok comes with all sorts of new considerations.
 The devotion and nonstop attention itself can be hard to contend with, especially for teens. They’re treated like media products, and addressed as such, without the preparation that an actual celebrity would have. Mooptopia ran up against the pressures of fame within the first few weeks of grasping it. An old picture of her standing in front of a Donald Trump sign made the horde  accuse her of secret MAGA loyalties; the natural curiosities of her enormous following led to amateur sleuths digging up her real name and the location of her high school; she showed up in a pervy subreddit dedicated to young TikTokers’ feet. And fans haven’t always liked the directions she’s taken in her videos. As she experimented with slightly more typical TikTok dance routines and glamour shots, they  complained: “Moop is slowly turning normal.” Just a couple of weeks into her unlikely stardom, seemingly in response to all of this, she called it quits by posting a  black screen with a text overlay reading in part, “It’s been a hard time for me lately and i must part away from tiktok.”
    Mooptopia came back only a few days after quitting TikTok. “Don’t ever leave us again queen,” her fans pleaded. Some fans have apologized for snooping, including the owner of an Instagram account dedicated to “rare” Moop pics from her childhood. “I guess i can’t be a detective,” they  wrote with a crying-cat emoji. “Sorry queen.” I was struck by the sincerity of the allegiance—something that was reserved for boy-band members and Disney Channel actors when I was a teenager.
 For parents, there are some obvious reasons to be terrified of the power and public attention a teenager can accrue in a few days on a social-media app they don’t totally understand. Audiences can be fickle and demanding and mean; adults can be creepy and dangerous; and teenagers can lack foresight—they might not want to be known forever for the things they say online as a minor. Take, for example, 15-year-old Claudia Conway, who turned into  an overnight star for posting to TikTok about her loathing for Trump and  her irritation with her parents, especially her mother, Kellyanne Conway, a former senior counselor to the president. The type of family spat that would normally happen at the dinner table was instead followed by millions of people. Claudia posted multiple times about needing a break from the circus to focus on her mental health, and her parents both quit their jobs shortly after her account blew up. (George and Kellyanne Conway declined to comment on the record for this story; Claudia did not respond to a request for comment.) Other TikTok stars have spoken about being  doxxed or  harassed after becoming famous.
  On top of that, parents could worry about the fact that the adulation of girls has rarely been equitable: As Kennedy pointed out in  a recent paper, many of the most followed girls on TikTok are young, white, and wealthy. Young Black creators have protested  their treatment on the app  for some time. The platform, as Kennedy put it, “renders some girlhoods hypervisible and others hidden in their shadows.”
 But what can make TikTok so scary to parents is precisely what makes it so appealing to their kids. “TikTok is a lingua franca. It’s a universal language among girls,” Pomerantz said. “It’s also a secret code, because adults often don’t understand what their daughters are doing. I think those two things—the connectivity and the secret language of it—are what make it so attractive.” For teens, the allure of TikTok isn’t just viral fame, but also the promise of world-building with their peers.
 TikTok is a massive network of girls talking primarily to one another, and by most available evidence, they understand what they’re doing. Mooptopia has enough followers to start thinking about endorsements and off-platform success, but has deliberately staked out a very different path for reasons only she knows. TikTok’s creator team says she isn’t working with any of its in-house growth strategists to establish a bigger career. She may have achieved viral fame, and she may have felt its sting as well, but her story is still a mystery, just the way she seems to like it.

title: 看看像《Mooptopia》这样的青少年TikTok明星，他们一下子病毒式走红，很难抗衡粉丝的投入和无止境的关注
title_en: A look at teen TikTok stars like “Mooptopia”, who achieved sudden viral fame and found it hard to contend with the devotion and nonstop attention of fans

一位科技公司的首席执行官在台上热情洋溢地解释说，Mac的到期日迫在眉睫。更重要的是，他即将向我们介绍一款为下一个十年设计的新电脑。在波士顿交响音乐厅，我坐在他2000多名观众中的一个遥远的座位上，空气中的期待之情足以引起高度的接触。
毕竟，我们是少数能直接从史蒂夫·乔布斯口中得知下一代电脑的幸运儿。
我们在1988年11月30日晚上看到的不是下一个发布会。七周前，在旧金山路易丝·M·戴维斯交响音乐厅，3000名受邀的开发人员、教育工作者和记者面前就发生了这件事。乔布斯现在正在波士顿计算机协会的月度大会上第二次表演同样的基本演示文稿。它对所有成员开放，因此比独家的旧金山版本更具公共性。
对于乔布斯来说，这是他三年多前被苹果公司赶下台以来的第一次产品发布会，赌注高得不能再高了。他的新电脑以大学市场为目标，由一些设计Mac的人发明，相当令人眼花缭乱。这是一个绰号为“立方体”的雄伟的1英尺长的黑色镁方块，它运行的是基于Unix的操作系统NeXTStep，并配备了一些创新功能，如用来存放你的应用程序的“底座”，以及用于构建新软件的拖放工具。从100万像素的高分辨率显示屏到256MB的大容量光存储驱动器，下一个立方体给人的感觉就像是未来，与最初的Macintosh几乎五年前的方式大致相同。(有一段时间，这是一个略显空洞的未来：NextStep 1.0直到1989年9月才发货，比原定计划晚了6个月，比乔布斯推出它晚了一年。)。
坐在那里被机器惊叹是一种奇怪的苦乐参半的经历。6500美元的价格远远超出了我的价格范围，想要一辆纯粹是一种渴望，就像渴望一辆兰博基尼一样。(我还不是一名科技记者，我没有理由指望我会以专业的身份接触到它--事实上，我现在仍然没有。)。但在傍晚结束时，当我们从乔布斯的现实扭曲领域涌出，回到波士顿寒冷的空气中时，我们每个人都有了下一个要带回家的产品：一张描绘立方体高不可攀的光泽海报。第二天早上，我把它钉在我的办公桌旁，并为没有保留它而后悔不已。
那个夜晚留在我的脑海里，是一个鲜活的--如果是空灵的--记忆。然而，我花了四分之一个世纪来调查我是否可以重新经历这一切。
当我问乔纳森·罗滕伯格(Jonathan Rotenberg)--他在1977年13岁时与人共同创立了波士顿计算机协会(Boston Computer Society)，当时该协会的主席将该协会变成了美国最大的用户群体--它是否制作了会议的视频，结果证明是：是的，有时会。令我高兴的是，这个调查帮助激发了一个项目，将这些古董视频数字化并分享。然而，下一次会议并不在被录像的会议之列。
然后一些不同寻常的事情发生了。我听查尔斯·曼(Charles Mann)的话，他在20世纪80年代和90年代初为数十次BCS会议和其他与计算机有关的活动录制了专业录音。我不知道，他当时以“权力分享系列”的名义卖出了许多录音带。1988年，当我正在享受乔布斯的演讲时，曼在大厅的其他地方录制它。下面是它的全部内容：
这只是个开始。曼恩在1983年9月的一个周末在纽约联合国国际学校(United Nations International School)举行的一个电脑博览会上录制了他的第一张唱片，演讲者阵容一众，如乔布斯的苹果联合创始人史蒂夫·沃兹尼亚克(Steve Wozniak)和人工智能大师西摩·帕普特(Seymour Papert)。“有30位令人惊叹的计算机先驱出席，在我看来，不记录这些事件几乎是对我们民族文化记忆的一种犯罪，”他回忆道。
在接下来的七年多时间里，曼恩在波士顿和纽约录制了演讲和演讲-通常是在一个给定的月里录制了几个演讲和演讲-加在一起构成了科技领域一个关键时代的音频历史。整个92个录音档案只需60美元就可以在USB驱动器上买到；由于有200小时的音频和16个多小时的视频，这是迄今为止无法买到的海量材料。
乔布斯录制了三张唱片-一张来自他在苹果公司的第一个任期，另两张来自下一届。比尔·盖茨在5号楼。有一些人已经是传奇人物(移动计算远见卓识的艾伦·凯，营销者非凡的雷吉斯·麦肯纳)，也有后起之秀(刚刚起步的个人电脑大亨迈克尔·戴尔，23岁)。从索尼(Sony)联合创始人森田昭夫(Akio Morita)到迷幻药物倡导者和兼职技术专家蒂莫西·利里(Timothy Leary)，每个人都有出席；仅仅是与苹果相关的材料，包括首席执行官约翰·斯卡利(John Sculley)谈论21世纪的公司，以及HyperCard的创建者比尔·阿特金森(Bill Atkinson)演示他的创意，都是一场盛宴。(从现在开始，本文中的音频片段是摘录的亮点，而不是整个演示文稿。)。
实际上，在古董录音中听到这些人，与阅读关于他们的故事，或者(如果你当时还活着，并像我一样专注于)依靠遥远的记忆，是一种完全不同的体验。仅举一个例子，专家兼企业家亚当·奥斯本(Adam Osborne)-他的名字在很大程度上被不公平地作为愚蠢地预先发布产品的同义词-得以在两段录音中用他帝王般的英国口音为自己辩护。在这里，他告诉BCS成员，随着计算机变得更强大，软件只会变得更令人讨厌，这一预测已经成为现实。
丹·布里克林(Dan Bricklin)与鲍勃·弗兰克斯顿(Bob Frankston)共同开发了第一个电子表格程序VisiCalc，他说：“你看看演讲者的名单，就会发现，这些东西必须永远保存在我们行业的档案中。”(他们的公司Software Arts资助拍摄一些BCS会议。)。布里克林参与了曼的三次录音，其中一次是1985年纽约个人电脑用户小组(New York PC Users Group)会议，他在会上讲述了VisiCalc的兴衰故事。
POWER SHARKING系列保留了很久以前的时间，而计算机书呆子们将陶醉于以曾经强大的名字为特色的会议，如雅达利、准将和Radio Shack。例如，1985年，在准将在纽约市宣布其开创性的Amiga电脑一个月后，总统汤姆·拉蒂根(Tom Rattigan)来到波士顿，向BCS成员展示了这台电脑，并辩称它让Mac相形见绌。
虽然这些录音可能很有趣，但它们的价值远远不是纯粹的怀旧。投资者埃丝特·戴森(Esther Dyson)表示：“现在似乎一切都被记录下来了，但在那些日子里，我们只是在探索和娱乐。”1985年，她发表了关于“人工知识”的演讲，当时她最出名的是个人电脑论坛(PC Forum)会议和1.0版时事通讯，这是曼恩记录的第一次BCS会议。“但重要的是，这些探索和乐趣归根结底是相当重要的。回顾过去，认识到即使技术本身在今天看起来可能相当原始，但人们已经很老练了，这总是很有用的。我们知道更多的事实，我们可以做更多的事情，但我不确定我们是否变得更明智了。“
虽然下一个立方体在交响乐厅的宏伟演示可能是波士顿计算机协会的终极会议，但它也是一个久负盛名的传统的一部分。多年来，各大电脑公司一直在利用该用户群体的月度大会向消费者推介新产品--包括1984年的麦金塔(Macintosh)，乔布斯在库比蒂诺的苹果年会上披露了这款产品几天后，就把它带到了波士顿。
1986年，也就是他离开苹果后的第二年，乔布斯来到波士顿，在哈佛商学院发表演讲，讲述他作为一名企业家的生活。谈话结束后，他在哈佛广场的一家酒吧与BCS总裁罗滕伯格聊天，并自豪地展示了著名设计师保罗·兰德以一本解释他的工作的书的形式交付给公司的Next标志。Rotenberg回忆道，当乔布斯向Rotenberg赠送这本书的副本时，NeXT闪亮的新标志“在我看来，它当时就像是史蒂夫·乔布斯宇宙的中心。也许只是因为这是他可以谈论的事情。“。
当时，下一台计算机本身仍然是一个未公布的谜。但罗滕伯格邀请乔布斯在准备好的时候在BCS会议上进行东海岸首映式。1988年秋天，他是。
就像1984年推出的Mac一样，BCS会议将是旧金山湾区正式发布的松散再现，除了在波士顿举行，并向普通公众开放-BCS成员和一定数量的随机感兴趣的团体，他们设法进入了BCS。随着乔布斯作为一个行业人物基本上消失了，他带着一台新电脑的重新出现成为了两个海岸的重大事件。罗滕伯格说：“仅仅是史蒂夫·乔布斯，他的下一件事，就让它本身就非常值得注意。”
根据兰德尔·斯特罗斯(Randall Stross)1993年出版的“史蒂夫·乔布斯与下一件大事”(Steve Jobs&amp；the Next Big Thing)一书，乔布斯选择在旧金山的戴维斯交响音乐厅(Davies Symphony Hall)推出Next，部分原因是为了展示这台机器令人印象深刻的音频能力--16位CD音质的音效，以及乔布斯所说的“随身听立体声耳机插孔”。除了魔方和一名人类小提琴家之间的二重唱，演示文稿还将展示额外的合成音乐，肯尼迪和马丁·路德·金(Martin Luther King Jr.)演讲的声音片段，以及一封电子邮件附带的语音录音演示。所有这些在当时都是令人兴奋的事情。
其他元素包括一部流畅的电影，展示了主板雄伟地穿过NeXT位于加利福尼亚州弗里蒙特的机器人控制的工厂，这是硬件迷恋视频的原型，这些视频后来成为苹果活动的主要内容。但就像乔布斯的任何主题演讲一样，主要的吸引力最终将是他和他的机器，因为他展示了公司已经建造了什么，它可以做什么。
戴维斯·霍尔事件的视频长期以来一直无法获得，这段视频是在2015年由亚伦·索尔金编剧的电影“史蒂夫·乔布斯”的制作过程中被发现的。(电影的一部分设置在活动的后台，尽管这是最远离历史重演的事情，首先是它已经从戴维斯音乐厅搬到了街对面的战争纪念歌剧院(War Memorial Opera House)。)。这段视频是由两盘录像带拼凑而成的，非常模糊，以至于在开头都写了一句话，表示希望能有更好的副本重新浮出水面。尽管如此，制造电影和结尾的新闻发布会(Look For Next Investors Ross Perot)状态更好，整个事情值得一看。
一旦乔布斯回到苹果，并开始定期做产品主题演讲，他们就以其无懈可击的优雅水平而闻名。相比之下，旧金山的NEXT活动并不是纪律的典范。首先，它跑了两个多小时-即使你是史蒂夫·乔布斯(Steve Jobs)，也要保持观众的参与度，这是一个很长的时间。(18年后，许多人认为这是有史以来最有效的一次产品发布会，他在1小时19分钟内发布了第一款iPhone。)
根据约翰·C·德沃夏克(John C.Dvorak)在“旧金山考官”(The San Francisco Examer)上的古怪描述，下一场活动晚了45分钟开始。乔布斯首先指出，“我已经有几年没有这样做了，所以我有点紧张。”后来，他跳过了准备好的关于Adobe的部分，解释说演示已经晚了，需要结束，这样大厅才能清理出来，以便当天下午有一场音乐会。
简而言之，戴维斯大厅的启动感觉就像是一份粗略的草稿。波士顿的版本将给NeXT一个机会来调整它，然后重新来一遍。
在波士顿，就像在旧金山一样，这座城市的主要音乐会场地将举办这场演出。除了它的音响优势，交响厅宽敞的空间可以容纳可能是有史以来举行的最大规模的用户-团体会议。人群控制是每个人的心头之事：1984年1月，当乔布斯在BCS的一次会议上向1100多人演示原版Mac时，另有300人被困在外面的雪地里。那是在活动在最后一刻被移到一个更大的大厅之后。
Rotenberg说：“这在我们的集体体验中是如此的灼热，人们被拒之门外，以至于我们真的，真的计划好了这次在交响厅的事情上。”“当人们到达时，我们会给他们不同颜色的卡片，这样他们就会按照到达的顺序进入。”
我们这些进入现场的人看到了一场不到90分钟的活动-基本上是对旧金山首映式的更严格的剪辑。它也有了一个更激动人心的开始，一段讨人喜欢的视频介绍了立方体的NeXTStep操作系统，这是超越基于文本的界面和Mac的下一步进步。这段视频由Keith Ohlfs制作，他的图标设计帮助定义了下一个界面-也是旧金山版的一部分，但它直到40分钟后才出现。这就是它-显然是一个比波士顿展示的更长的版本，因为它没有与音频同步：
我很高兴这段视频保存得如此完好。但曼恩的录音令人震惊的一点是，它在没有视觉成分的情况下工作得很好。在我听它之前，我假设纯音频版本最多只能有视频的一半收益。不是这样的：它本身是可以理解的，也是有价值的，尽管你看不到吸引着所有BCS成员的计算机。
也许这并不令人惊讶。毕竟，史蒂夫·乔布斯作为一名演讲者的力量主要来自于他说的话和说的方式，而不是他看起来的样子。这是可以测试的：试着在声音关闭的情况下观看任何乔布斯的演示文稿，然后再次使用音频，但没有图片。您觉得哪个版本更有说服力？
曼认为自己是听众的“现场特工和‘看得见眼’的向导”，当仅从配乐中看不清一些东西时，他确实偶尔会在他的录音带上提供有用的感叹。但当他录制演示文稿时，他发现它们并不需要太多的评论：“当演讲者将他的电脑屏幕投影到屏幕上时，他通常会讲述我们正在看的东西，所以通常不需要额外的旁白。”
事实上，当你看不到乔布斯展示的东西时，即使是在乔布斯演示软件的活动中，也有令人惊讶的连贯性-比如在这一节中介绍了NeXTStep的突破性用户界面。
在昏暗的大厅里(如旧金山Next发布会)拍摄科技行业活动的复古视频，从来都不是一种精确的高清体验，有时甚至根本无法辨认。相比之下，权力分享音频节目通常听起来就像是昨天录制的。这一事件尤其如此：虽然曼恩和他的音响师约翰·卡梅隆(也是PBS的Nova)通常不得不安装自己的麦克风，但下一个视听团队允许他们将盘到盘的录音机插入主系统。“随着团队下一步在那里管理声板，我们得到了一个真正干净的音频馈送，”曼恩说。
对曼和我们来说，幸运的是，当晚最吸引人的部分是最响亮的听觉部分。尽管第一代苹果电脑在那个时代确实拥有相当强大的音效，但在乔布斯1984年发表的发布会中，人们只短暂地提到了它。然而，对于NeXT，乔布斯想要强调立方体产生音乐的能力-不仅因为这很酷，而且还想从更高的层面上说明NeXT想要打造的不仅仅是一个纯粹的计算设备。
在旧金山和波士顿演讲接近尾声时，乔布斯引用了宝丽来创始人埃德温·H·兰德的话，埃德温·H·兰德是他心目中的英雄之一。乔布斯解释说：“他说，‘我希望宝丽来能站在艺术和科学的交叉点上’，我永远不会忘记这一点，这也是我们许多人对下一步的期望。”然后他介绍了一位交响乐小提琴家，这位小提琴家演奏了巴赫的A小调小提琴协奏曲，作为与下一个立方体的二重奏。(这可能是一个巧合，但兰德也以将现场古典音乐融入他主持的活动而闻名。)。
所有这些都预示着，一旦乔布斯回到苹果，“史蒂文诺特”就会降临。他有时在演讲接近尾声时说，苹果试图坐在“科技和文科的交汇点”，这是对兰德声明的扭曲。特别是当涉及iPod时，苹果经常以乔布斯推出音乐表演而告终，从U2到坎耶·韦斯特(Kanye West)。这样的表演变得如此熟悉，以至于当发射结束时没有一次发射时，一些人感到震惊。
然而，回到1988年，在电脑发布会上的音乐表演是一件新奇的事情，甚至在你意识到这是人与机器之间的合作之前就是如此。我仍然记得坐在交响音乐厅里，感受到乔布斯一定想要产生的那种敬畏。
在下一个立方体的西海岸首次亮相时，旧金山交响乐团的小提琴家丹尼尔·科比亚尔卡(Daniel Kobialka)进行了表演。为了举办波士顿版的活动，NeXT联系了波士顿爱乐乐团的首席小提琴手，后者最终推荐了助理首席小提琴手海琳·波尔(Helene Pohl)担任这份工作。“当时我还是一名学生，所以非常令人兴奋，”波尔回忆道，他已经担任新西兰弦乐四重奏首席小提琴手超过25年。她说，乔布斯最初对表演的想法包括下一步监控她的演奏，并相应地调整自己的角色；当事实证明这是不现实的时，她不得不让计算机带头。
“当然，从那以后，技术有了很大的进步，”波尔说。“我确实觉得这是对未来的一瞥。考虑到2020年音乐创作的社会距离，这是一个有先见之明的想法。“
在聘请波尔在交响厅表演后，NeXT还让她飞到旧金山，与魔方一起为科佩尔报道(Koppel Report)的一集“来自地球的新闻”(News From Earth)进行二重唱。科佩尔报道是由Nightline的泰德·科佩尔(Ted Koppel)主持的一系列美国广播公司(ABC)黄金时段新闻特别节目。该节目于1988年12月26日播出，作为一种时间胶囊，为任何有朝一日可能接手广播的外星人提供了好处。它以理查德·尼克松、德斯蒙德·图图、简·古德尔、让-米歇尔·库斯托和史蒂文·旺德等名人为特色。还有史蒂夫·乔布斯(Steve Jobs)，他用NeXT的语录电子书演示了他的下一次演讲的部分内容的袖珍版，并摘录了波尔的表演。
这是乔布斯、波尔和魔方，最后还有一段史提夫·旺德(Stevie Wonder)的片段。请注意，乔布斯从来没有提到下一个人的名字(“嗨，我是史蒂夫·乔布斯，我制造电脑。”)；据推测，ABC对整个事件都很敏感，因为这是免费的商业时间。
乔布斯在BCS的下一个演示的音频-以及其他几十个录音-之所以存在，完全是因为曼恩在35年前就意识到，在计算机用户组会议和会议上进行的谈话正在创造历史。作为一名发展经济学家，他大部分时间在其他国家帮助当地人制定食品政策，他最初对计算机感兴趣，是因为它们有可能帮助他在突尼斯的工作。这导致他在1982年联合制作了一段关于Apple II和VisiCalc的视频。因为他相信个人电脑是关于电源和共享的，所以他把这个项目称为“电源共享系列”(Power Sharing Series)。
第二年，曼恩参加了由大苹果用户组和纽约IBM个人电脑用户组共同主办的联合国国际学校的计算机展览会。由于活动的组织者全神贯注地确保一切都按计划进行，他们同意让他录制演讲以供分发。1985年2月，曼开始录制其他技术聚会-许多波士顿计算机协会会议，以及纽约用户组秘密会议和在HARVA举行的活动。
